title: caladaptaer
text:
If you work in R and you have tried to pull California’s downscaled climate projections off the Cal-Adapt Analytics Engine, you have probably hit a wall. The data sits as cloud optimized Zarr on AWS S3, and until now the R side of that story basically did not exist. caladaptaer is the piece that was missing. You ask for a variable, a model, a scenario, and a time window, and it hands back a native R object, a stars grid or a tidy data.frame, ready to drop into the rest of your work. No Python, no API key, nothing to authenticate.
And it stays general on purpose. caladaptaer is a data gateway, not a model. Whatever you do in R, ecology, hydrology, ag, energy, water resources, carbon, you point it at the projection you need and keep working in your own tools.

title: caladaptaer
text:
How it works flowchart LR classDef data fill:#F6A23C,stroke:#C77B1E,color:#1f1300,stroke-width:1px; classDef fn fill:#BBD8EC,stroke:#3B7DB5,color:#0f3350,stroke-width:1px; classDef next fill:#EAF3FB,stroke:#3B7DB5,color:#0f3350,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray:5 3; classDef goal fill:#ffffff,stroke:#555555,color:#111111,stroke-width:1px; subgraph DATA["Data, level by level"] direction TB S["Cal-Adapt on AWS S3
WRF and LOCA2 Zarr stores"]:::data R["native R object
stars grid or data.frame,
one variable"]:::data A["analysis ready
harmonized across
WRF and LOCA2"]:::data D["model drivers
CF NetCDF"]:::data G(["your science
any domain"]):::goal S --> R --> A --> D --> G A --> G end subgraph CODE["caladaptaer"] direction TB DISC["Discover
cae_catalog, cae_models, cae_scenarios,
cae_variables, cae_search, cae_check_variables"]:::fn FETCH["Fetch, the engine
cae_fetch, cae_fetch_point, cae_fetch_points"]:::fn HARM["Harmonize and stamp provenance
next up"]:::next DRV["Drivers
cae_write_cf_netcdf, cae_build_met_drivers"]:::fn end DISC -.->|"what is on S3"| S FETCH -->|"GDAL /vsis3/, no Python, no key, no auth"| R HARM --> A DRV --> D The Analytics Engine stores its climate data as Zarr, a cloud native array format. A Zarr store is not one giant file you download whole; it is split into many small chunks, and a reader can pull just the chunks it needs over the network. caladaptaer reads those chunks through GDAL’s /vsis3/ virtual filesystem, the same engine already sitting under sf, stars, and terra. That is why there is nothing to install on the Python side, no key to manage, and no client session to keep alive. You ask for what you want and the bytes come back as an R object.

title: caladaptaer
text:
The data you can reach
Two downscaling products live on the Engine, both downscaled from CMIP6 global models for California and the western US.











Dataset
Resolution
Step
GCMs
Scenarios
Period




WRF CMIP6 (WUS-D3)
45 / 9 / 3 km
Hourly
8
SSP3-7.0 (all); SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 (CESM2)
1980–2100


LOCA2-Hybrid
3 km
Daily
15
SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0, SSP5-8.5
1950–2100



They get there two different ways, and which one you want depends on the job. WRF is dynamically downscaled by UCLA (Rahimi et al. 2024), a physics based weather model run at high resolution, so it carries the full hourly surface meteorology and resolves things like mountain rain shadows and the sea breeze. Reach for it when you need hourly drivers or process based physics. LOCA2 is statistically downscaled by Scripps (Pierce et al.), mapping coarse model output onto a fine grid from historical analog patterns, so you get daily data for more models and more scenarios at 3 km. Reach for it when you want a wide ensemble or daily extremes. caladaptaer reads both, plus the ERA5 reanalysis driven WRF run, through the same handful of functions.

title: caladaptaer
text:
What’s in the box
Eleven cae_* functions cover the whole path from catalog to model ready files. You can search the CADCAT catalog by model, scenario, variable, and resolution, and check up front whether a model and scenario even carry the fields you need. You can read any Zarr store into a stars grid, or pull a time series at one point or ten thousand; the multi site read grabs the grid from S3 once and extracts every site locally, so it is one read per variable, not one read per site. And you can turn WRF output into CF-1.8 NetCDF for ecosystem models, units and all, in a single call. Precipitation gets filled in automatically for the models that ship it split across rainc and rainnc.

title: caladaptaer
text:
Quick start
remotes::install_github("lebauerapproach/caladaptaer")
library(caladaptaer)

# what is on the engine
cae_models(activity = "WRF")

# does CESM2 SSP3-7.0 carry the variables I need
cae_check_variables("CESM2", "ssp370")

# a summer day of hourly temperature at Fresno, 2050
cae_fetch_point("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00",
                end_time   = "2050-07-01T23:00:00")
Getting Started takes this from install to a real time series, Data Access is the full tour of every function with live output, and Showcase answers “what can I actually do with it” in a few worked analyses against live data.

title: caladaptaer
text:
Used in production
We run caladaptaer in our MAGiC project to pull climate projections for our California design points, where it has already generated 12,474 site-years of hourly drivers: three GCM and scenario combinations (CESM2 SSP2-4.5, CESM2 SSP3-7.0, MPI-ESM1-2-HR SSP3-7.0) over 2025 to 2045.

title: caladaptaer
text:
Where we’re headed
Today caladaptaer nails access: find the data, read it, turn it into model ready files. Three things come next. First, an analysis ready layer that harmonizes WRF and LOCA2 behind one interface and quietly applies Cal-Adapt’s known data quirks (the renamed humidity and wind variables, the precipitation unit fix), so you write code once and run it on both. Second, provenance: every output stamped with exactly where it came from, the store, version, slice, model, scenario, and transform, plus the Cal-Adapt citation carried through, so you can rerun and cite a file straight from itself. Third, the analysis layer that turns raw projections into what people actually report on, spatial subsetting to counties and watersheds, temporal aggregation, derived variables like growing degree days and reference ET, warming levels, extremes, and bias correction. After that, rOpenSci review and a CRAN submission.
Why it is worth doing: it is the only real path into the Analytics Engine for R users right now, since the old R client stops at the previous CMIP5 generation. The provenance piece is something even the Python tooling does not do today. And it stays general, so the same gateway serves an ecologist, a hydrologist, or an energy modeler. We are building it to sit next to the Python tools in the Cal-Adapt ecosystem, not to fork them.

text:

title: caladaptaer
text:
Citing and license
caladaptaer is released under BSD-3-Clause. See CITATION.cff in the repo for how to cite it.

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
What you will learn




How to prepare CF-standard met driver files for hundreds of sites in a single pass using cae_build_met_drivers()
Why the bulk approach is dramatically more efficient than looping over cae_fetch_point()
How to set up a sites table and configure the output for use with PEcAn or standalone ecosystem model runs

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Background
Ecosystem modeling projects often need met drivers at many sites – the CCMMF project, for example, runs SIPNET at 198 design points across California. Preparing met files one site at a time with cae_fetch_point() is straightforward but wasteful: each call reads the entire WRF grid from S3 independently, so 198 sites x 8 variables x 20 years means 31,680 S3 requests.
cae_build_met_drivers() solves this by calling cae_fetch_points() (plural) under the hood. The full WRF grid is read from S3 once per variable per year, and all sites are extracted from that grid in a single vectorized operation. The same 198-site, 20-year job now takes 160 S3 requests.

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Preparing the sites table
The input is a data.frame with three required columns: site_id, lon, and lat. Any additional columns are ignored.

# read CCMMF design points
sites <- read.csv("design_points.csv")
head(sites)
#>   UniqueID          site_id      lat       lon          pft
#> 1  1301797 8773c58306e8d9a0 32.71585 -115.4716 annual crop
#> 2  1300446 f2ce12d75f3b2cc6 32.75557 -115.4159 annual crop
#> ...

# cae_build_met_drivers needs site_id, lon, lat
# the site_id column is already present in this table

If your sites come from PEcAn’s BETY database or another source, just make sure you have those three columns:

# manual setup for a few sites
sites <- data.frame(
  site_id = c("fresno", "la", "sacramento"),
  lon = c(-119.77, -118.24, -121.49),
  lat = c(36.75, 34.05, 38.58)
)

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Building the drivers

library(caladaptaer)

result <- cae_build_met_drivers(
  sites     = sites,
  model     = "CESM2",
  scenario  = "ssp370",
  start_year = 2050,
  end_year   = 2055,
  outdir    = "met_drivers"
)

Progress output looks like:
Year 2050 (1 of 6)
  fetching t2
Reading grid (1 S3 fetch for all 3 sites)
Extracting 3 sites
  fetching psfc
  ...
  wrote met_drivers/fresno/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc
  wrote met_drivers/la/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc
  wrote met_drivers/sacramento/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc
Year 2051 (2 of 6)
  ...
The result is a data.frame tracking what was written:

head(result)
#>    site_id year                                        file
#> 1   fresno 2050   met_drivers/fresno/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc
#> 2       la 2050       met_drivers/la/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc
#> 3 sacramento 2050 met_drivers/sacramento/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Output file structure
Each NetCDF file contains 9 CF-standard variables at hourly resolution for one site and one year:



CF variable
Units
Source




air_temperature
K
t2 (direct)


air_pressure
Pa
psfc (direct)


precipitation_flux
kg m-2 s-1
prec / 3600


specific_humidity
1
q2 / (1 + q2)


surface_downwelling_shortwave_flux_in_air
W m-2
swdnb (direct)


surface_downwelling_longwave_flux_in_air
W m-2
lwdnb (direct)


eastward_wind
m s-1
u10 (direct)


northward_wind
m s-1
v10 (direct)


wind_speed
m s-1
sqrt(u10^2 + v10^2)



Dimensions are latitude(1), longitude(1), and time(unlimited). The time coordinate uses seconds since {year}-01-01 00:00:00.

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Skipping existing files
By default, cae_build_met_drivers() skips sites that already have output files for a given year. If all sites in a year are complete, the S3 fetch for that year is skipped entirely. Pass overwrite = TRUE to regenerate everything.

# re-run after interruption -- only missing files are written
result <- cae_build_met_drivers(
  sites, model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
  start_year = 2050, end_year = 2055,
  outdir = "met_drivers"
)

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Verifying the output
Spot-check a file to make sure values are physically reasonable:

library(ncdf4)

nc <- nc_open("met_drivers/fresno/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc")
t2 <- ncvar_get(nc, "air_temperature")
pr <- ncvar_get(nc, "precipitation_flux")
nc_close(nc)

# temperature: 200--350 K
range(t2)

# precip flux: 0--0.01 kg/m2/s (0--36 mm/hr, reasonable max for hourly)
range(pr)

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Efficiency comparison
For 198 sites x 8 variables x 20 years:








Approach
S3 requests
Description




cae_fetch_point() loop
31,680
one grid read per site per var per year


cae_build_met_drivers()
160
one grid read per var per year


PEcAn download.CalAdaptWRF
160
same count, uses tempdir() cache



All three produce identical output. The choice depends on whether you are working inside PEcAn (download.CalAdaptWRF) or outside it (cae_build_met_drivers).

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Multiple models and scenarios
To build drivers for multiple GCMs or scenarios, loop over the combinations:

models <- c("CESM2", "CNRM-ESM2-1", "EC-Earth3-Veg")

for (m in models) {
  cae_build_met_drivers(
    sites, model = m, scenario = "ssp370",
    start_year = 2050, end_year = 2069,
    outdir = file.path("met_drivers", m)
  )
}

CESM2 supports SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 in addition to SSP3-7.0. All other GCMs have SSP3-7.0 only.

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
References

Rahimi, S. et al. (2024). An overview of the Western United States Dynamically Downscaled Dataset (WUS-D3). Geoscientific Model Development, 17, 2265–2286. doi:10.5194/gmd-17-2265-2024
Dietze, M. C. et al. (2014). A quantitative assessment of a terrestrial biosphere model’s data needs across North American biomes.

title: Accessing Cal-Adapt Data in R
text:
This is the full tour. Every exported function in caladaptaer, with a real call and the output it gives back, all framed around a mid century window (July 2050, SSP3-7.0) so the examples hang together. If you just want to get a time series on screen in a couple of calls, start with Getting Started and come back here when you want the whole surface.
The functions fall into three groups: discovery (what is on S3 and where), fetching (pull grids and points into R), and drivers (turn WRF output into CF standard NetCDF for ecosystem models). Argument notes here are short on purpose. The full signature for every function, with defaults and return types, lives in the package reference.

title: Accessing Cal-Adapt Data in R
section: What is on the Analytics Engine
text:
What is on the Analytics Engine
The Cal-Adapt Analytics Engine hosts about 1 PB of downscaled CMIP6 climate projections for California and the western US, produced for the state’s Fifth Climate Change Assessment. The data sits in cloud optimized Zarr stores on AWS S3, public, no credentials. caladaptaer reads those stores straight into R through GDAL’s /vsis3/ virtual filesystem, so there is no Python, no API key, and nothing to authenticate.
Two downscaling products are on offer:
WRF CMIP6 (WUS-D3), dynamically downscaled by UCLA (Rahimi et al. 2024). The Weather Research and Forecasting model runs hourly at three nested grids: 45 km (d01, western US), 9 km (d02, California and surroundings), and 3 km (d03, California). Eight GCMs are downscaled, mostly under SSP3-7.0, with CESM2 also carrying SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5. There is also an ERA5 reanalysis driven run. WRF gives you the full surface meteorology: temperature, pressure, precipitation, humidity, radiation, and wind.
LOCA2-Hybrid, statistically downscaled by Scripps (Pierce et al.). Daily output at 3 km for 15 GCMs across three SSPs, covering 1950 to 2100.

title: Accessing Cal-Adapt Data in R
text:
Setup

library(caladaptaer)
library(stars)
library(sf)
library(maps)

The Zarr driver needs GDAL 3.4 or newer:

sf::sf_extSoftVersion()["GDAL"]
#>     GDAL 
#> "3.11.5"

title: Accessing Cal-Adapt Data in R
text:
Discovery

The catalog
cae_catalog() pulls the CADCAT index, one row per variable per model/scenario/resolution combo, and caches it for the session. Pass refresh = TRUE to force a fresh download.

cat <- cae_catalog()
nrow(cat)
#> [1] 8416
names(cat)
#> [1] "activity_id"    "institution_id" "source_id"      "experiment_id" 
#> [5] "member_id"      "table_id"       "variable_id"    "grid_label"    
#> [9] "path"

The columns follow CMIP6 naming. This is the vocabulary every other function speaks:



Column
Meaning
Examples




activity_id
Downscaling method
WRF, LOCA2


source_id
Parent GCM
CESM2, MIROC6, EC-Earth3


experiment_id
Scenario
ssp370, ssp245, historical


table_id
Temporal resolution
1hr, day, mon


variable_id
Variable short name
t2, prec, pr, tasmax


grid_label
Spatial grid
d01 (45 km), d02 (9 km), d03 (3 km)


member_id
Ensemble member
r1i1p1f1


path
S3 Zarr store
s3://cadcat/wrf/ucla/cesm2/…





Models, scenarios, variables
Three thin helpers read straight off the catalog so you do not have to filter it by hand. Each takes an activity of "WRF" or "LOCA2" (or NULL for both).

cae_models(activity = "WRF")
#> [1] "CESM2"         "CNRM-ESM2-1"   "EC-Earth3"     "EC-Earth3-Veg"
#> [5] "ERA5"          "FGOALS-g3"     "MIROC6"        "MPI-ESM1-2-HR"
#> [9] "TaiESM1"


cae_models(activity = "LOCA2")
#>  [1] "ACCESS-CM2"      "CESM2-LENS"      "CNRM-ESM2-1"     "EC-Earth3"      
#>  [5] "EC-Earth3-Veg"   "FGOALS-g3"       "GFDL-ESM4"       "HadGEM3-GC31-LL"
#>  [9] "INM-CM5-0"       "IPSL-CM6A-LR"    "KACE-1-0-G"      "MIROC6"         
#> [13] "MPI-ESM1-2-HR"   "MRI-ESM2-0"      "TaiESM1"

cae_scenarios() also takes a model to narrow it down. CESM2 carries the widest set on the WRF side:

cae_scenarios(activity = "WRF", model = "CESM2")
#> [1] "historical" "ssp245"     "ssp370"     "ssp585"

cae_variables() takes a timescale ("1hr", "day", "mon"). The hourly WRF list is long because it includes the full radiation and hydrology fields:

cae_variables(activity = "WRF", timescale = "1hr")
#>  [1] "dew_point"            "effective_temp_index" "ffwi"                
#>  [4] "lwdnb"                "lwdnbc"               "lwupb"               
#>  [7] "lwupbc"               "prec"                 "psfc"                
#> [10] "q2"                   "rainc"                "rainnc"              
#> [13] "rh"                   "runsb"                "runsf"               
#> [16] "snow"                 "snownc"               "swddif"              
#> [19] "swddir"               "swddni"               "swdnb"               
#> [22] "swdnbc"               "swupb"                "swupbc"              
#> [25] "t2"                   "tsk"                  "u10"                 
#> [28] "v10"                  "znt"



Searching
cae_search() filters the catalog on any combination of activity, model, scenario, variable, timescale, and resolution. Everything you pass is ANDed together.

cae_search(activity = "WRF", model = "CESM2",
           variable = "t2", timescale = "1hr", resolution = "d01")
#>      activity_id institution_id source_id experiment_id member_id table_id
#> 3441         WRF           UCLA     CESM2    historical r11i1p1f1      1hr
#> 3667         WRF           UCLA     CESM2        ssp245 r11i1p1f1      1hr
#> 3854         WRF           UCLA     CESM2        ssp370 r11i1p1f1      1hr
#> 4080         WRF           UCLA     CESM2        ssp585 r11i1p1f1      1hr
#>      variable_id grid_label                                              path
#> 3441          t2        d01 s3://cadcat/wrf/ucla/cesm2/historical/1hr/t2/d01/
#> 3667          t2        d01     s3://cadcat/wrf/ucla/cesm2/ssp245/1hr/t2/d01/
#> 3854          t2        d01     s3://cadcat/wrf/ucla/cesm2/ssp370/1hr/t2/d01/
#> 4080          t2        d01     s3://cadcat/wrf/ucla/cesm2/ssp585/1hr/t2/d01/



Checking a driver set before you fetch
Before you commit to building met drivers for a model and scenario, ask whether the variables you need are actually there. cae_check_variables() takes a model and scenario and reports, for the eight fields SIPNET wants, whether each is directly available or derivable.

cae_check_variables("CESM2", "ssp370")
#>   variable available derivable note
#> 1       t2      TRUE     FALSE     
#> 2     psfc      TRUE     FALSE     
#> 3     prec      TRUE     FALSE     
#> 4       q2      TRUE     FALSE     
#> 5    swdnb      TRUE     FALSE     
#> 6    lwdnb      TRUE     FALSE     
#> 7      u10      TRUE     FALSE     
#> 8      v10      TRUE     FALSE

derivable flags variables built from others, like total precipitation summed from its convective and non convective parts. A clean run of TRUE in the available column means you are good to go.

title: Accessing Cal-Adapt Data in R
text:
Fetching

A grid
cae_fetch() reads a Zarr store from S3 and hands back a stars object. Keep the first read small by windowing with start_time and end_time (ISO strings or POSIXct); a one hour window is one timestep. resolution picks the grid and timescale picks 1hr (WRF) or day (LOCA2).

t2 <- cae_fetch("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00", end_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00")
t2
#> stars object with 3 dimensions and 1 attribute
#> attribute(s):
#>            Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max.
#> t2 [K] 275.6917 291.9042 296.4104 297.2769 302.4306 319.2205
#> dimension(s):
#>      from  to         offset delta  refsys x/y
#> x       1 109       -6307614 45000 unnamed [x]
#> y       1 104       -1148589 45000 unnamed [y]
#> time    1   1 2050-07-01 UTC    NA POSIXct

Three dimensions come back: x and y in the WRF Lambert Conformal grid, and time in UTC. Values are 2 meter air temperature in Kelvin.


Mapping it
The 45 km domain covers most of western North America. Drop state lines on top to orient the map.

western <- st_as_sf(
  map("state", c("california", "oregon", "washington", "nevada",
                 "arizona", "utah", "idaho", "montana", "wyoming",
                 "colorado", "new mexico"), plot = FALSE, fill = TRUE)
)
ca <- st_as_sf(map("state", "california", plot = FALSE, fill = TRUE))
western_wrf <- st_transform(western, st_crs(t2))
ca_wrf <- st_transform(ca, st_crs(t2))

t2_c <- t2
t2_c[[1]] <- units::drop_units(t2[[1]]) - 273.15

plot(t2_c, main = "", axes = TRUE, reset = FALSE,
     col = hcl.colors(100, "YlOrRd", rev = TRUE),
     key.pos = 4, key.width = lcm(1.5), key.length = 0.8)
plot(st_geometry(western_wrf), add = TRUE, border = "grey30", lwd = 0.6)
plot(st_geometry(ca_wrf), add = TRUE, border = "black", lwd = 1.5)




For California only work at 9 or 3 km, pass resolution = "d02" or "d03", but mind the memory: one 3 km timestep is about 150 MB against 0.3 MB at 45 km.


A point
cae_fetch_point() reads the grid and pulls the time series at the nearest cell to a lon/lat. The transform from WGS84 to the WRF Lambert grid is handled for you. Here is a summer day at Fresno:

ts <- cae_fetch_point("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                      lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                      start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00",
                      end_time   = "2050-07-01T23:00:00")
head(ts)
#>                  time    value
#> 1 2050-07-01 00:00:00 304.5662
#> 2 2050-07-01 01:00:00 302.7805
#> 3 2050-07-01 02:00:00 301.1025
#> 4 2050-07-01 03:00:00 298.4392
#> 5 2050-07-01 04:00:00 295.5602
#> 6 2050-07-01 05:00:00 293.1013

You get a plain data.frame of time and value, so base plotting works right away.

ts$temp_c <- ts$value - 273.15
plot(ts$time, ts$temp_c, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "#D62728",
     xlab = "time", ylab = "temperature (C)")






Many points in one read
cae_fetch_points() is the design choice worth knowing. It reads the grid from S3 once and extracts every site locally in a single pass. Three sites or ten thousand, it is one S3 read per variable, not one read per site. Pass a data.frame with lon and lat columns (and an optional site_id).

sites <- data.frame(
  site_id = c("davis", "fresno", "sacramento"),
  lon = c(-121.74, -119.77, -121.49),
  lat = c(38.54, 36.75, 38.58)
)
mp <- cae_fetch_points("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                       points = sites,
                       start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00",
                       end_time   = "2050-07-01T05:00:00")
#> Reading grid (1 S3 fetch for all 3 sites)
#> Extracting 3 sites
head(mp, 8)
#>   site_id                time    value
#> 1   davis 2050-07-01 00:00:00 304.7827
#> 2   davis 2050-07-01 01:00:00 303.4674
#> 3   davis 2050-07-01 02:00:00 301.8932
#> 4   davis 2050-07-01 03:00:00 299.8713
#> 5   davis 2050-07-01 04:00:00 297.6558
#> 6   davis 2050-07-01 05:00:00 295.5995
#> 7  fresno 2050-07-01 00:00:00 304.5662
#> 8  fresno 2050-07-01 01:00:00 302.7805



Comparing models
Because every GCM rides the same downscaling framework, lining them up at one location shows the inter model spread, a read on structural uncertainty in the projections.

wrf_370 <- Filter(function(m) "ssp370" %in% cae_scenarios(activity = "WRF", model = m),
                  setdiff(cae_models(activity = "WRF"), "ERA5"))

model_ts <- lapply(wrf_370, function(m) {
  d <- cae_fetch_point("t2", model = m, scenario = "ssp370",
                       lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                       start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00",
                       end_time   = "2050-07-01T23:00:00")
  d$model  <- m
  d$temp_c <- d$value - 273.15
  d
})
model_ts <- do.call(rbind, model_ts)

cols <- hcl.colors(length(wrf_370), "Dark 3")
plot(model_ts$time, model_ts$temp_c, type = "n", xlab = "time", ylab = "temperature (C)")
for (i in seq_along(wrf_370)) {
  d <- model_ts[model_ts$model == wrf_370[i], ]
  lines(d$time, d$temp_c, col = cols[i], lwd = 2)
}
legend("topright", legend = wrf_370, col = cols, lwd = 2, cex = 0.7, bty = "n")






The full surface meteorology
SIPNET and most ecosystem models want several fields at once. The hourly WRF data carries all of them. Here are the seven SIPNET inputs at Fresno, each one its own cae_fetch_point() call:



WRF name
Field
Units




t2
2 meter air temperature
K


psfc
Surface pressure
Pa


prec
Precipitation
mm/timestep


q2
Water vapor mixing ratio
kg/kg


swdnb
Downward shortwave radiation
W/m2


u10
10 meter eastward wind
m/s


v10
10 meter northward wind
m/s




vars <- c("t2", "psfc", "prec", "q2", "swdnb", "u10", "v10")
met <- lapply(vars, function(v) {
  cae_fetch_point(v, model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                  lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                  start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00",
                  end_time   = "2050-07-01T23:00:00")
})
names(met) <- vars
sapply(met, function(d) round(mean(d$value), 2))
#>       t2     psfc     prec       q2    swdnb      u10      v10 
#>   294.56 99909.53     0.00     0.01   363.65     1.99    -4.09

title: Accessing Cal-Adapt Data in R
text:
Drivers
The last two functions turn fetched WRF output into CF standard NetCDF that ecosystem models read directly. These write files to disk, so they are shown here without running.

One site, hand assembled
cae_write_cf_netcdf() takes a named list of stars objects (names are WRF variable IDs), does the unit work (precipitation to flux, mixing ratio to specific humidity, U and V to wind speed), and writes one CF-1.8 file. dt_seconds sets the timestep length, default 3600 for hourly.

var_list <- list(
  t2    = cae_fetch("t2",    model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                    start_time = "2050-01-01", end_time = "2050-12-31"),
  psfc  = cae_fetch("psfc",  model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                    start_time = "2050-01-01", end_time = "2050-12-31"),
  prec  = cae_fetch("prec",  model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                    start_time = "2050-01-01", end_time = "2050-12-31")
  # ... and the rest of the SIPNET set
)
cae_write_cf_netcdf(var_list, outfile = "fresno_2050.nc", dt_seconds = 3600)



Many sites, end to end
cae_build_met_drivers() is the production path. Hand it a data.frame of sites (site_id, lon, lat), a model, scenario, and a year range, and it fetches every required variable once, extracts all sites, converts units, and writes one CF NetCDF per site per year under outdir. resolution defaults to d01; overwrite and verbose do what they say.

sites <- data.frame(
  site_id = c("site_0001", "site_0002", "site_0003"),
  lon = c(-121.74, -119.77, -121.49),
  lat = c(38.54, 36.75, 38.58)
)
cae_build_met_drivers(sites,
                      model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                      start_year = 2050, end_year = 2050,
                      outdir = "met/", resolution = "d01")

This is the engine behind the production runs. For the full driver pipeline, walked step by step, see SIPNET Met Drivers, and for scaling it across many sites see Bulk Met Drivers.

title: Accessing Cal-Adapt Data in R
text:
Where to go next

SIPNET Met Drivers – raw WRF to CF standard NetCDF
Bulk Met Drivers – the multi site driver path
Package reference – every function, every argument, every default

title: Accessing Cal-Adapt Data in R
text:
References

Rahimi, S. et al. (2024). An overview of the Western United States Dynamically Downscaled Dataset (WUS-D3). Geoscientific Model Development, 17, 2265–2286. doi:10.5194/gmd-17-2265-2024
Pierce, D. W. et al. Statistical downscaling using Localized Constructed Analogs (LOCA). Journal of Hydrometeorology, 15(6), 2558–2585.
Cal-Adapt Analytics Engine: https://analytics.cal-adapt.org/
CADCAT on AWS: https://registry.opendata.aws/caladapt-coproduced-climate-data/

title: Getting Started
text:
caladaptaer reads downscaled CMIP6 climate projections off the Cal-Adapt Analytics Engine directly into R. Data lives as cloud optimized Zarr on AWS S3, and the package reads it through GDAL: no Python, no API key, no authentication. Every call hands back a native R object, a stars grid or a tidy data.frame, so results drop right into the R geospatial and modeling stack.
This page goes from install to a real time series in a few calls, then shows the design choice that lets the same code scale to thousands of sites. Every output below comes from actually running the code on this page. For the full function tour see Accessing Cal-Adapt Data in R.

title: Getting Started
text:
Install

remotes::install_github("lebauerapproach/caladaptaer")

You need R >= 4.1 and GDAL >= 3.4 for the Zarr driver. Confirm your GDAL is recent enough:

sf::sf_extSoftVersion()["GDAL"]
#>     GDAL 
#> "3.11.5"


library(caladaptaer)

title: Getting Started
text:
Discover what’s available
Ask what the WRF dynamical downscaling covers:

cae_models(activity = "WRF")
#> [1] "CESM2"         "CNRM-ESM2-1"   "EC-Earth3"     "EC-Earth3-Veg"
#> [5] "ERA5"          "FGOALS-g3"     "MIROC6"        "MPI-ESM1-2-HR"
#> [9] "TaiESM1"

Eight GCMs plus the ERA5 reanalysis driven run. CESM2 carries the widest set of scenarios:

cae_scenarios(activity = "WRF", model = "CESM2")
#> [1] "historical" "ssp245"     "ssp370"     "ssp585"

title: Getting Started
section: Pull a time series at a point
text:
Pull a time series at a point
cae_fetch_point() reads the grid and extracts the nearest cell to a lon/lat. Transform from WGS84 to the WRF Lambert Conformal grid happens for you. Here’s a summer day in 2050 at Fresno under SSP3-7.0:

ts <- cae_fetch_point("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                      lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                      start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00",
                      end_time   = "2050-07-01T23:00:00")
head(ts)
#>                  time    value
#> 1 2050-07-01 00:00:00 304.5662
#> 2 2050-07-01 01:00:00 302.7805
#> 3 2050-07-01 02:00:00 301.1025
#> 4 2050-07-01 03:00:00 298.4392
#> 5 2050-07-01 04:00:00 295.5602
#> 6 2050-07-01 05:00:00 293.1013

Result is a plain data.frame with time and value in Kelvin, so base R plotting just works. Times are UTC, so the midday dip is the local overnight low (UTC noon is roughly 5am Pacific).

ts$temp_c <- ts$value - 273.15
plot(ts$time, ts$temp_c, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "#D62728",
     xlab = "time", ylab = "temperature (C)")

title: Getting Started
section: Extract many locations in one read
text:
Extract many locations in one read
This is the design choice worth knowing up front. cae_fetch_points() reads the grid from S3 once and extracts every location locally in a single vectorized pass. Three sites or ten thousand, it’s one S3 read per variable, not one per site.

sites <- data.frame(
  site_id = c("davis", "fresno", "sacramento"),
  lon = c(-121.74, -119.77, -121.49),
  lat = c(38.54, 36.75, 38.58)
)
mp <- cae_fetch_points("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                       points = sites,
                       start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00",
                       end_time   = "2050-07-01T05:00:00")
#> Reading grid (1 S3 fetch for all 3 sites)
#> Extracting 3 sites
head(mp, 8)
#>   site_id                time    value
#> 1   davis 2050-07-01 00:00:00 304.7827
#> 2   davis 2050-07-01 01:00:00 303.4674
#> 3   davis 2050-07-01 02:00:00 301.8932
#> 4   davis 2050-07-01 03:00:00 299.8713
#> 5   davis 2050-07-01 04:00:00 297.6558
#> 6   davis 2050-07-01 05:00:00 295.5995
#> 7  fresno 2050-07-01 00:00:00 304.5662
#> 8  fresno 2050-07-01 01:00:00 302.7805

title: Getting Started
text:
Where to go next

Accessing Cal-Adapt Data in R – every function with output: catalog search, gridded fetch, California maps, multi model comparison, and the full surface meteorology
SIPNET Met Drivers – raw WRF to CF standard NetCDF for ecosystem models
Bulk Met Drivers – the multi site extraction path

title: Showcase
text:
Quick answer to “what can I actually do with this thing.” Three questions you’d really ask about California’s climate future, each one knocked out in a few lines of R straight off live Cal-Adapt data.
Plots aren’t really the point. Point is, grabbing this data into R, which used to be hours of grief, is already handled, so you get to spend your time on science instead.

title: Showcase
section: One interface, two very different products
text:
One interface, two very different products
Analytics Engine ships two downscaled products, and they flat out don’t line up. WRF is dynamical, hourly, sits on a 45 km Lambert grid, and calls near surface air temp t2. LOCA2 is statistical, daily, on a 3 km lat/lon grid, and doesn’t even carry a mean temperature, just daily tasmax and tasmin. Different physics, different grid, different time step, even different variable names.
caladaptaer hands you both back as one tidy data.frame, so you can just throw them on the same plot. Here’s the same GCM (EC-Earth3), SSP3-7.0, at Fresno, one week of July 2050, pulled from both.

# WRF: hourly t2, 45 km
wrf <- cae_fetch_point("t2", model = "EC-Earth3", scenario = "ssp370",
                       lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                       start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00",
                       end_time   = "2050-07-07T23:00:00")

# LOCA2: daily tasmax and tasmin, 3 km, same model and spot
tmax <- cae_fetch_point("tasmax", model = "EC-Earth3", scenario = "ssp370",
                        lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                        timescale = "day", resolution = "d03",
                        start_time = "2050-07-01", end_time = "2050-07-07")

tmin <- cae_fetch_point("tasmin", model = "EC-Earth3", scenario = "ssp370",
                        lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                        timescale = "day", resolution = "d03",
                        start_time = "2050-07-01", end_time = "2050-07-07")

head(wrf, 3)
#>                  time    value
#> 1 2050-07-01 00:00:00 311.8828
#> 2 2050-07-01 01:00:00 311.5065
#> 3 2050-07-01 02:00:00 311.3383
head(tmax, 3)
#>                  time    value
#> 1 2050-06-30 12:00:00 312.8626
#> 2 2050-07-01 12:00:00 313.3137
#> 3 2050-07-02 12:00:00 313.4529

Same call both times. Past the variable name, only thing that changes is timescale = "day" and resolution = "d03" to aim at LOCA2 store instead of WRF one.

# kelvin to C
wrf$c  <- wrf$value  - 273.15
tmax$c <- tmax$value - 273.15
tmin$c <- tmin$value - 273.15

plot(wrf$time, wrf$c, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "grey30",
     ylim = range(c(wrf$c, tmax$c, tmin$c)),
     xlab = "time", ylab = "temperature (C)")
lines(tmax$time, tmax$c, col = "#D62728", lty = 2); points(tmax$time, tmax$c, pch = 19, col = "#D62728")
lines(tmin$time, tmin$c, col = "#1F77B4", lty = 2); points(tmin$time, tmin$c, pch = 19, col = "#1F77B4")
legend("topright", bty = "n", cex = 0.8,
       legend = c("WRF t2 (hourly)", "LOCA2 tasmax (daily)", "LOCA2 tasmin (daily)"),
       col = c("grey30", "#D62728", "#1F77B4"),
       lwd = c(2, 1, 1), lty = c(1, 2, 2), pch = c(NA, 19, 19))




And yeah, they disagree, that’s the honest read. WRF overnight lows run cooler than LOCA2 tasmin. Part of it is that 45 km WRF cell smears over way more, and way more varied, ground than that little 3 km LOCA2 cell, and part of it is that dynamical and statistical downscaling are just different animals. Bottom line, which product you grab changes your number. Pulling both into R side by side is how you actually see that for yourself instead of taking somebody’s word for it.
This is what we’re chasing next: one interface that lines up names, units, and time across both products, and quietly handles the known Cal-Adapt gotchas (those renamed humidity and wind variables, that precip unit fix) so you never trip over them. Home page has where that fits.

title: Showcase
section: How much does the model you pick matter
text:
How much does the model you pick matter
Eight GCMs get dynamically downscaled in WRF. They all run through the same downscaling machinery, so stacking them up at one spot gives you a clean read on structural uncertainty: how much of your answer is actual climate, versus just which model spit it out.

wrf_370 <- Filter(
  function(m) "ssp370" %in% cae_scenarios(activity = "WRF", model = m),
  setdiff(cae_models(activity = "WRF"), "ERA5")
)

series <- lapply(wrf_370, function(m) {
  d <- cae_fetch_point("t2", model = m, scenario = "ssp370",
                       lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                       start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00",
                       end_time   = "2050-07-01T23:00:00")
  d$model <- m
  d$c <- d$value - 273.15
  d
})
series <- do.call(rbind, series)

cols <- hcl.colors(length(wrf_370), "Dark 3")
plot(series$time, series$c, type = "n", xlab = "time", ylab = "temperature (C)")
for (i in seq_along(wrf_370)) {
  d <- series[series$model == wrf_370[i], ]
  lines(d$time, d$c, col = cols[i], lwd = 2)
}
legend("topright", legend = wrf_370, col = cols, lwd = 2, cex = 0.7, bty = "n")




Models agree on the shape, cool night, hot afternoon, but they fan out hard on the actual temperature, 10 C or more apart at the afternoon peak (the hottest, MPI-ESM1-2-HR, tops 44 C while the coolest sits near 30). Clock is UTC, so that midday dip is really the local overnight low. Anything you run downstream that cares about temperature, a crop model, a fire index, a building load, an ecosystem carbon flux, that spread is the error bar you’re stuck with just from picking a model. Pushing the whole ensemble through to a calibrated answer is on our roadmap. Grabbing that ensemble in one place, today, is what makes it doable.

title: Showcase
text:
What only hourly data shows
LOCA2 and the raw GCMs are daily at best. WRF is hourly, and that cracks open something nothing else can: the shape of the day. Heat stress, evapotranspiration, nighttime respiration, they all key off the sub daily swing, not the daily mean. So here’s that swing at three California landscapes over a summer stretch in 2050.

sites <- data.frame(
  name = c("Coast (San Francisco)", "Central Valley (Fresno)", "Sierra (Mammoth area)"),
  lon  = c(-122.42, -119.77, -119.03),
  lat  = c(  37.77,   36.75,   37.63),
  col  = c("#1F77B4", "#D62728", "#2CA02C")
)

series <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(sites)), function(i) {
  d <- cae_fetch_point("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                       lon = sites$lon[i], lat = sites$lat[i],
                       start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00",
                       end_time   = "2050-07-03T23:00:00")
  d$c <- d$value - 273.15
  d$name <- sites$name[i]
  d
})
series <- do.call(rbind, series)

plot(series$time, series$c, type = "n", xlab = "time", ylab = "temperature (C)")
for (i in seq_len(nrow(sites))) {
  d <- series[series$name == sites$name[i], ]
  lines(d$time, d$c, col = sites$col[i], lwd = 2)
}
legend("topright", legend = sites$name, col = sites$col, lwd = 2, cex = 0.8, bty = "n")




Coast barely budges, ocean pins it in a tight band. Central Valley swings the widest. Sierra site is coldest at every hour. That’s exactly the physics you’d expect, and it drops out of three cae_fetch_point() calls. No daily product can show you that.

title: Showcase
section: Build a variable that isn’t in the file
text:
Build a variable that isn’t in the file
Cal-Adapt hands you wind as two components, eastward u10 and northward v10, not as a single speed. No problem, grab both and combine them. Here’s the 10 m wind at Fresno over a couple of July days.

# eastward and northward components
u <- cae_fetch_point("u10", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370", lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                     start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00", end_time = "2050-07-02T23:00:00")
v <- cae_fetch_point("v10", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370", lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                     start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00", end_time = "2050-07-02T23:00:00")

# wind speed is just the magnitude
ws <- data.frame(time = u$time, speed = sqrt(u$value^2 + v$value^2))

plot(ws$time, ws$speed, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "#2C7FB8",
     xlab = "time", ylab = "wind speed (m/s)")




Same trick works for anything you can build off the raw fields, vapor pressure deficit, a heat index, reference ET. The parts are all in there.

title: Showcase
section: Zoom out to the whole state
text:
Zoom out to the whole state
Points are one thing, but you can read the whole grid and clip it to whatever region you care about. Here’s a summer afternoon over California: pull the 45 km WRF field, clip it to the state, and you have a map and a number, the statewide average, out of the same object.

library(stars)
library(sf)
library(maps)

# one 45 km timestep over the western US
g <- cae_fetch("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370", resolution = "d01",
               start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00", end_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00")

# California polygon, into the WRF grid's projection
ca <- st_transform(st_as_sf(map("state", "california", plot = FALSE, fill = TRUE)), st_crs(g))

# clip the grid to the state, kelvin to C
g_ca <- g[ca]
g_ca[[1]] <- units::drop_units(g_ca[[1]]) - 273.15

# statewide average out of one aggregate call
round(as.numeric(aggregate(g, ca, FUN = mean, na.rm = TRUE)[[1]])[1] - 273.15, 1)
#> [1] 27.6


plot(g_ca, reset = FALSE, axes = TRUE,
     col = hcl.colors(100, "YlOrRd", rev = TRUE),
     key.pos = 4, key.width = lcm(1.4), key.length = 0.8, main = "")
plot(st_geometry(ca), add = TRUE, border = "black", lwd = 1.2)




That’s 45 km, so California comes out to about 191 cells, blocky but honest. The statewide mean falls out of one aggregate() call. Want a county or a watershed instead? Swap the polygon, everything else stays the same.

title: Showcase
text:
Where this is going
Everything up there uses only what the package does today: read a point or a grid, across both products, in a few calls. Here’s where it goes from here.

Analysis ready across products. One interface that harmonizes WRF and LOCA2 (names, units, grid, time) and applies the known Cal-Adapt data fixes automatically, so write once and run across both.
Provenance you can reproduce from. Every output stamped with the exact store, version, slice, model, scenario, and transform that made it, and with the Cal-Adapt citation carried through, so a file can be rerun and cited from itself.
The analysis layer. Spatial subsetting to counties and watersheds, temporal aggregation, derived variables, warming levels, and carrying the full ensemble through to calibrated uncertainty. Home page has the full roadmap, and Data Access walks every function with live output.

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
What you will learn




Which meteorological variables SIPNET requires and where they come from in the WRF dataset
How WRF native units map to CF standard names and units
How to download all 7 variables and write a CF-compliant NetCDF file

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
Background
SIPNET is a process-based ecosystem model that simulates carbon, water, and energy fluxes at hourly timesteps. It expects met drivers in CF-standard NetCDF format.
The Cal-Adapt WRF hourly output is a natural fit for SIPNET met forcing under future climate scenarios. This vignette walks through the pipeline: query the catalog, read all variables, convert units, and write a NetCDF.

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
Variable mapping
SIPNET needs 5 meteorological inputs plus wind components. The full CF-standard set also includes longwave radiation for other ecosystem models:










CF standard name
WRF name
WRF units
CF units
Conversion




air_temperature
t2
K
K
none


air_pressure
psfc
Pa
Pa
none


precipitation_flux
prec
mm/timestep
kg/m2/s
divide by dt


specific_humidity
q2
kg/kg (mixing ratio)
kg/kg (specific)
q = w/(1+w)


surface_downwelling_shortwave_flux_in_air
swdnb
W/m2
W/m2
none


surface_downwelling_longwave_flux_in_air
lwdnb
W/m2
W/m2
none


eastward_wind
u10
m/s
m/s
none


northward_wind
v10
m/s
m/s
none



Most need no conversion. Precipitation goes from accumulated mm per timestep to an instantaneous flux rate (dividing by 3600 for hourly data). Humidity goes from mixing ratio to specific humidity – the difference is small but worth getting right.

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
Download all variables

library(caladaptaer)

sipnet_vars <- c("t2", "psfc", "prec", "q2", "swdnb", "lwdnb", "u10", "v10")

var_data <- lapply(sipnet_vars, function(v) {
  message("reading ", v)
  cae_fetch(v, model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370", n_timesteps = 24)
})
names(var_data) <- sipnet_vars

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
Inspect the data
Always spot-check that values fall in physically reasonable ranges before feeding them to an ecosystem model.

# temperature: 200--350 K
summary(var_data$t2[[1]])

# pressure: 50000--110000 Pa
summary(var_data$psfc[[1]])

# shortwave: 0--1400 W/m2
summary(var_data$swdnb[[1]])







Sanity checks



WRF output is model output, not observations. Temperature below 200 K, negative shortwave, or negative pressure indicate a data problem – don’t pass those through to the model.

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
Write CF-standard NetCDF
cae_write_cf_netcdf() handles unit conversion and CF metadata in one call:

cae_write_cf_netcdf(
  var_list = var_data,
  outfile = "CESM2.ssp370.2014-09-01.cf.nc",
  dt_seconds = 3600,
  overwrite = TRUE
)

The output includes CF-1.8 global attributes, standard variable names, and computed wind speed. The output is ready for SIPNET ensemble runs.

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
Point extraction alternative
For single-site runs, extract time series at a specific location instead of writing the full grid:

pts <- lapply(sipnet_vars, function(v) {
  cae_fetch_point(v, model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                 lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75, n_timesteps = 24)
})
names(pts) <- sipnet_vars




Figure 1: Hourly meteorological variables at Fresno, CA from the CESM2 SSP3-7.0 WRF simulation. These are the inputs SIPNET needs for a single-site carbon flux simulation. The diurnal signatures are physically consistent – peak radiation drives peak temperature with a lag, humidity drops as temperature rises, and pressure follows a semidiurnal tide.

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
